insight: Scorchilla's early version (7GR, buildtime 1) was significantly stronger than the current version (3GR, buildtime 3) because the low build time allowed immediate, powerful burst attack pressure comparable to a Bombard. The intermediate version (5GR, buildtime 2) was considered weak and had to be buffed to the current stat line.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about whether current Scorchilla is overpowered, with historical version comparison provided by mrguy888 who played all three versions on ladder.

insight: Build time is a non-linear factor in unit strength: increasing build time by 1 on a cheap unit (e.g., 3GR Scorchilla to 5GR buildtime 2) causes a disproportionately large power loss, because the longer build time reduces the relevance of each individual purchase and makes the unit clunkier to fit into opening build orders.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 explaining why 5GR buildtime 2 Scorchilla was weaker than either 7GR buildtime 1 or 3GR buildtime 3 versions.

insight: Scorchilla's low gold-to-tech ratio makes it valuable compared to Tarsier in sets where spending green tech is otherwise difficult; buying Tarsier instead can leave excess green unspent, effectively lowering the value of your tech and raising Scorchilla's relative value. Simple efficiency models that compare attack-per-gold fail to capture this context-dependent advantage.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 critiquing the use of inflation-model ROI numbers to compare Scorchilla and Tarsier without accounting for tech-spending constraints.

insight: When two players both have access to the same cheap attacker (e.g., 3GR Scorchilla), neither player can easily push the other off buying it, whereas with a more expensive first-buyer version (7GR Scorchilla), the first buyer raises the value of their own reds and lowers the opponent's reds by making supply scarcer — a strategic asymmetry that inflation models do not capture.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 pointing out a flaw in RiFT's ROI spreadsheet comparison of the three Scorchilla versions.

insight: mrguy888 has long considered Grimbotch in need of a nerf, and masn6811 suggested a minimal nerf of reducing its Click attack by 1 (i.e., Click: Gain 0 Attack) as sufficient to address its power level, particularly its P2 advantage.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion comparing the polarizing nature of shock units and Grimbotch's P2 coinflip potential.

insight: masn6811 observed that nearly every unit with the Shock mechanic was either nerfed or had Shock removed during balance patches, even though theoretically paying for attack in gold versus resources should not matter much. The exception is Redeemer, which is the only unit that gained Shock from a balance change.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion about shock units and historical balance trends following the Scorchilla version history thread.

insight: The Prismata base set is designed to fulfill two specific roles: (1) guarantee you can always spend any single tech resource on either a permanent attacker or prompt HP, and (2) set a baseline power floor. It is intentional that base-set units alone should not counter two advanced set units — if they did, the advanced set would be failing its design purpose.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 explaining to Zera why the lack of base-set counterplay to Hellhound + Electrovore is by design, not a balance problem.

insight: Valkyrion synergizes with units that 'hide attacks' — i.e., units that generate attack which resolves to 0 before the turn ends — allowing Valkyrion to trigger its ability without the player needing visible excess attack. It can also function as absorb-denial, though this is weak against units like Xae, Wall, or Electrovore.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 explaining Valkyrion's synergies in the context of a set where the opponent chose Valkyrion without supporting units.

insight: Feral Warden effectively provides Wall-equivalent absorb on a red attacker, removing the need to buy Blastforge for defense in sets where Wall is the best blue unit. However, its value as an absorber is only realized if the player continuously buys one per turn; failing to do so creates a severe defense shortfall that collapses small-economy games quickly.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion of whether Feral Warden is overtuned, with shadourow explaining the conditions under which its absorb value is realized.

insight: Feral Warden is primarily a low-to-mid inflation unit: in high-inflation games, permanent attackers lose value because they cannot keep up with the pace of the game, and players prefer units with the highest immediate defensive pressure. Wall provides 3 HP immediately while Feral provides only 1 HP immediately, making Wall relatively better when tempo is at a premium.
author: darkeisbein
context: Debate about whether Feral Warden's absorb value increases at high inflation, with darkeisbein and jamberine arguing it does not.

insight: A gold-stealing unit that removes up to X gold from the opponent violates Shalev's rule because having more gold worsens the opponent's outcome (they lose more), meaning the opponent is incentivized to have fewer resources — a broken game-state incentive. A correctly designed version would always give the acting player X gold regardless of how much the opponent has.
author: velizar_
context: Discussion of a hypothetical gold-stealing unit design and whether it would violate Shalev's rule.

insight: Blood Pact has niche midgame utility beyond its primary use: it can provide resource granularity, enable buying units like Valkyrion without committing blue to Wall, and combos well with chill units. It was historically stronger when it gave the opponent a Tarsier (old version) but was nerfed so it is now worse in the situations where it was previously bought.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion of Blood Pact's historical versions and its current niche uses including synergy with chill.

insight: The inflation-model ROI spreadsheet (by apooche/RiFT) assigns 3GR Scorchilla 35.2% ROI, 5GR Scorchilla 34.5% ROI, and 7GR Scorchilla 37.1% ROI, but mrguy888 argues the model is inaccurate because the values of variables (e.g., attack value, tech value) change depending on which version is being evaluated, and the model does not account for supply dynamics or build-order constraints that differ between versions.
author: mrguy888
context: RiFT posted inflation model ROI numbers for three Scorchilla versions; mrguy888 challenged the methodology.

insight: Centurion is a genuinely efficient absorber, but it is possible to win games with no Centurion against an opponent who has one, indicating it is not an auto-win condition. However, any unit or mechanic that deals permanent damage to absorbers (such as a hypothetical 'burn' attacker) would effectively hard-counter Centurion and D Grid, making those absorbers significantly weaker in such sets.
author: velizar_
context: Discussion of Feral Warden's efficiency and a hypothetical burn mechanic that permanently damages absorbers.
